sentences.text: Yes, I'm not completely certain.
There were certain rations that the Germans-- I remember honey, for instance, that I hated at the time.
Probably because there was some of it that-- and sugar somehow was collected.
Otherwise, there were-- I suppose my mother, because she worked in the kitchen, was in charge of it.
She probably was small able to get some kind of usually pasta.
I remember we ate a lot of-- for us.
And sometimes we went to that soup kitchen that she ran.
And I think that my family, the uncles, had some money.
It was-- there were-- food was being smuggled in into the ghetto.
And probably they were in a position to get more than some people.
This was still before the liquidation of the ghetto.
They were working in the-- the-- the factory was working.
They were even making item and little, you know, Madonnas, and metal things like that.
So it must have been somehow sold, you know, the Catholic Poles.
I-- I don't know.
So I think they were in a position-- probably in a much better position than other people.
So I-- I remember we were not particularly hungry.
We more or less ate the same things.
I know my cousin, he was the favorite grandson.
And somehow when he was-- yes, he was allowed a piece of chicken and an orange, so I know that must have been unusual.
You earlier on that to begin with in the ghetto, things were-- I mean, you have relatively good memories of it.
Did-- do you remember things getting worse?
Or-- or--
I think that probably not for me and my sister till this order that-- that-- Jews had to-- we were going to be exported out.
And-- and the knowledge as to what has been happening.
I don't think that I particularly noticed.
But maybe that I-- just things just became worst slowly.
I didn't know-- because at first, yes, we had some lessons.
Somebody came and gave us reading lessons.
But all that stopped quite quickly.
And there were even libraries in the ghetto.
I remember secret libraries.
I remember secret libraries.
We at first used to go.
Secretly we were not to tell people where they were.
And also, I think some parcels came to the ghetto.
There was some way through Portugal.
I think the Jewish Labor Committee, from America, I think had some arrangements.
And because my uncle was in America, I think some food came, some-- I think sardines and [INAUDIBLE] occasionally.
It was-- but I remember the children smuggling and being shot.
And so-- because where we lived on, [INAUDIBLE] street, it was on the edge of the ghetto.
And there was a hole where children would smuggle.
Usually they would get away with it.
But sometimes they were shot.
Yes, yes, yes.
Yes.
I do remember we-- I remember eating some bread in the street and children suddenly-- some-- which I think happened a lot, just pulled it out of our hands.
And ate-- immediately put it straight into their mouth, and they ate it.
And-- and also yes, there was typhoid.
And-- and you know, lice, even then.
And then-- yes, we were a bit protected from that.
And people, really, yes-- I remember people were dead on the street from hunger.
And some of the children I went to school with had these very huge bellies.
Then we would be told that they died.
Yes.
I know that we were not asked.
But before that, my mother did try to make a contact with some Polish family.
They were very close friends of my uncle who is now in America.
And they were very friendly with that.
The grandparents, they knew them.
And at some point, very early on, they offered to have my grandmother stay with them.
But she then refused.
But they took quite a lot of the stuff.
They had, I don't, fur coats and some jewelery and things to keep safely as they thought, till the end of the war.
So my mother rang them to ask if they would have me and my sister.
It was pleasant, there were telephones in the ghetto.
But I know that she phoned them.
Because she was upset because they said it's out of the question for them to receive her.
So there was beginning to be a sort of thought of the-- that should find someway to leave the ghetto.
I knew that.
And then-- so I wasn't surprised when I was told that we would be taken out.
I remember my grandfather being very worried that I will become a Christian.
[INAUDIBLE].
I don't remember much about saying goodbye to anyone.
But I remember my grandfather.
At the time, I still remember saying to him, oh such a silly story.
I couldn't.
And I remember him saying cleverer people than you [LAUGHS] have become Christian-- who are Christians.
I remember that.
And then I remember it was night time.
We were taken.
Sort of-- it was, I think, a kind of regular smuggling out place, where the Germans who were-- who were guarding the wall of the ghetto were bribed.
And we were-- I know that [?
Marek Edelman ?] was one of the young people on guard.
He's the-- one of the surviving ghetto leaders, ghetto [INAUDIBLE] leaders.
And there was a lot to put up.
Both sides of the wall.
One up, one down.
I remember being told to run up and down quickly.
And with Michal, who have not met.
And--
Yes, yes--
No, no.
No, just my sister.
Yes.
The offer was just for the children.
Yes, for us.
And I remember immediately because I was not sort warned this-- what might happen.
But I realized that-- Michal sort of sensed that the people in the town was sort of looking at us and recognizing that we were Jewish, perhaps, probably-- I mean, obviously.
And he told us to jump off the-- with him off the tram.
And to catch another one, then another one.
And to sort of [INAUDIBLE].
No one warned me about this indication that's related to the local people.
Not that-- that we had to hide so strongly that we were Jewish.
No, it wasn't exciting.
I-- well, we were suddenly around strangers people I didn't know and-- and-- and worried-- knew what probably happened to the rest of the family.
So yes, it-- but Michal had so much charm that he did-- and then he was prepared to talk to us and talk about himself.
And made it-- much-- much easier.

And he took us to a little flat somewhere on the outskirts of Warsaw where he lived himself with-- in a tiny room, really, with the-- I think he was the caretaker of the factory were Michal had worked before the war.
And we all stayed there.
There were two sons of this family.
They were called Dubil, mother and father.
This was the first Polish family that we stayed--
--we came with Michal.
No, no.
He was living-- He was living there as well at first but for very short time.
And he was making these bottles with different liquids in it and telling us all about-- you know, how he was going to fight.
And it all seemed quite exciting for me.
I-- I remember being quite, at first, quite excited by all this.
They somehow-- they were incinerating bottles like Molotov cocktails.
They somehow were, because they were thrown.
They'd explode.
And one of the sons of the Dubils was helping him smuggle these things into the ghetto.
They had some connection with other people.
And the Dubils, the family-- they were very nice elderly people.
They were not Jewish, no, no.
And I remember they gave us some food, which we at first couldn't eat because we were not used to eating sort of eggs and milk and things.
I remember being so sick.
We got used to it, yes.
And then Michal moved out of there.
And we were still together with the Dubil family.

And then the Dubils knew a Jewish family still in the ghetto who gave them money to somehow, I don't know, to buy or rent a flat, a bigger flat, where they would come as well and be hidden by the Dubils.
Well, at first we saw that the war was suddenly being surrounded by much-- by many more Germans.
And this-- they [? were ?] all the different uniforms.
The Lithuanian squad, whatever they were, who-- and [INAUDIBLE] and Latvians, and some Polish police as well.
And then shootings we could hear and then some tanks even went through the-- into the ghetto.
We could see that.
And then very soon after that, we saw the Germans pouring petrol into the houses.
And the houses began to burn.
And people coming out sort of really burning, their clothes burning and shouting, screaming.
And being shot at.
And, you know, the whole thing suddenly-- I mean, all my family was still in the ghetto.
In fact, we used to see my mother before that.
She used to come to the other side of the wall.
And we would sit in the window and gesture to her.
And-- so suddenly--
Yes, yes, yes.
So then suddenly, there was this kind of huge inferno, just smoke and-- and it was a lot of burning paper, I remember, sort of flying in the-- and-- and-- you see?
And there's-- yes, by that time, it was [?
Vladka Patitel, ?] who she became sort of responsible for me and my sister to pay [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this monthly money that these people were being paid.
Yes, yes.
I don't know how much that was.
And she became friends.
She was friendly with the family.
So she-- at that time, she came quite a lot.
Because she also wanted to see what was happening in the ghetto.
It was-- she could see through that window.
And that-- and she was very-- she was very brave.
She tried sort of-- was-- She was young and a very nice looking and [? was ?] going down and talking one of the Ukrainians, kind of flirting with him, to try to persuade him to go in.
She wanted to go into the ghetto to see what's happening.
To go-- but she didn't succeed in the end.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So yes, she was-- she was around.
And--
I don't know where she lived.
That was the secret.
I never knew where she lived.
We didn't really know of anyone else, but-- but she came.
She was part of the underground, yes.
She was one of those official couriers.
She was delivering money to other people who were in hiding.
And-- And-- so-- but then-- and also the other thing, was a terrible thing, that we saw was that the-- some of the Poles became very, very frightened.
It was terribly frightening.

The ones who had little children who perhaps then thought that the families had all been killed, they all [INAUDIBLE] just took these children and put to them next to the ghetto wall and the Germans just shot them.
And [?
Vladka ?] was also talking about how she used to try to save one of these children, but they didn't succeed.
Jewish children.
But they were Polish families who I suppose had offered to help these Jewish children hiding them.
But suddenly, they-- they-- I don't know.
They just-- those children just suddenly appeared.
We saw sort of one, but [?
Vladka-- ?] I remember she told us that she saw more.
And--
Well, this was on the the sort of third or fourth floor.
And yes, we were trying to pretend that we were not Jewish.
And I don't know if people guessed or not.
I remember some people came from-- who said they were from the [INAUDIBLE], this is the Polish underground army, to-- to-- to take some films of the ghetto.
Because it was such a big-- good venue.
And sort of looking at the me and-- and-- and saying something, oh, it's a very risky business having these children here, to-- to the Dubils.
So they obviously recognized what-- what we were, because we were Jewish.
And then the Dubils-- and also one of the cousins who used to help with smuggling some of the armaments into the ghetto, he somehow turned to become an informer.
And he tried to get some money from the Dubils.
[INAUDIBLE] suceed and then [INAUDIBLE] the Gestapo came, looking for us.
But we happened to been taken out by Mrs.
Dubil to church that day.
So when the-- so we went.
And after that, they were frightened with both of us.
So I had to go.
So they told [?
Vladka ?] that they would keep my sister but not me.
I don't know, [INAUDIBLE].
I don't know, but they found it easier to get on with her or something.
But, yes, they did.
I don't know.
So, yes, I suppose I was a little difficult, perhaps more miserable of the two, I don't know.
[LAUGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so-- and then some other place was found for me in the countryside.
That was the second family.
I was taken there by [?
Vladka ?] by train.
But I can't remember where it was.
I don't know where it was.
It was a family-- poor family where the man, the husband, had some job on the railways.
And they some tiny, a little small holding with a few chickens and were very hungry.
And there was a girl my age who was terribly-- you know, she was very unpleasant to me and I think told other children that I was Jewish.
So and also remember some story how the chicken got run over by the train, and that was a sort of sign that, you know, that God or-- was not in sympathy of them having me.
So, quite quickly, I had to leave then.
Vladka came again and took me to another family called the Serafins.
And that was the third family.
And also very near the ghetto wall.
I came back, yes, yes.
It must have been quite-- those seem very long.
But I would only imagine perhaps six weeks.
No, just the girl who was always taunting me.
The father was very nice to me.
And the mother was a little-- I don't know, she was-- I don't know, she was like a very poor kind of woman.
Who was just trying to find some food, I suppose.
And lots of-- other smaller children.
She was [? having ?] a baby, I think at, the time.
I don't remember much about that.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] Yes, I mainly remember this girl who I had first thought might be my friend or something.
And-- and it didn't work out that way.
And so I came to this other family, and they were, again probably most-- also kind very deranged, in a way, family, very-- very poor.
They-- they were sons of a caretaker.
And especially the men that I stayed with, his wife and baby.
He was-- he was young he was only about 23 or 24.
But he was a type of alcoholic.
And he used to really by comparison, to be quite nice to me.
He was a terrible drunk and beating and threading his wife with knives and hanging himself.
I mean, never succeeding, only probably meaning to.
So, I stayed there, really.
Yes, it was just next to the wall.
And he used to go in with some gang of people.
And they used to take out from the ghetto the-- kind of whatever they are called.
The parts of the building.
The-- kind of heavy.
The guarders, yes.
And somehow sell them.
And he did all sorts of things that were sort of illegal.
I mean, he had me and got paid for that.
And then, they were baking bread.
They had an oven, and that was, I guess, a very risky thing to do.
And they made [NON-ENGLISH].
And there were always somebody coming.
And they had to pay money to-- for these kind of informants to go away.
Yes, it was.
Well, hmm, I don't know.
They probably sold some.
They certainly sold the bread.
They were baking bread for sale.
Yes, I-- yes, oh yes.
Oh yes, I-- I mean, looking back on it over many, I [? was also ?] quite bitter.
I think they treated me probably-- you know, they were just very difficult with each other.
And so it wasn't easy to be there among them.
But-- but they were quite nice to me, looking back on it, considering all the circumstances.
They had a little girl.
A daughter.
She was about two.
Two years old, yes.
I also I had to look after her, yes.
Well, they moved out somehow.
I didn't know where they where.
There was some-- because I had stayed with them, they didn't want me to come back.
Because they had told some story that I had gone to my family somewhere out of Warsaw.
I didn't-- we didn't-- they moved out of there.
And I never went to visit my sister.
But she came to visit the me very occasionally at the Serafins.
I remember sort of-- because we still had some of the same clothes.
How-- and sometimes she would wear something that I had.
And that seemed-- you know, risky business.
Well, I was very happy to see her.
[LAUGHS]
She didn't used to come in very much, would sort of stay in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE] as well.
No, I was-- that was something I couldn't even bear to think.
I-- I must have assumed everyone was killed.
Because I'd seen so many people killed.
But-- I-- I-- I just didn't know what happened and didn't know for years and years what happened.
But I know now.
Well, years later, I found a-- a letter that my father was sent by someone who was with my mother in Majdanek with really the description how she became ill and was taken into the gas chamber.
So she survived the ghettos, came out, of course, must have been taken by the Germans, and sent to Majdanek, which I think a lot of people-- some people from Warsaw.
And there was very few people from Warsaw who did survive, less than people from other towns.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- but yes, I suppose at the end of the war, I was still thinking she might come back.
Yes.
No, no, no.
She was just the one child.
Well, not she.
I mean, she wasn't-- she was rather victim.
I think he was.
Well, he was always drunk.
And sort of out of control.
Oh, in that one room all these things were happening.
We were all sleeping and eating and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- yes, and [?
Vdlaka ?] and then-- [?
Ben Meed ?] was now her husband.
He also came once or twice.
When-- she was arrested at some point by the Gestapo.
And he came to pay for me.
Once a month.
And then some other people were also-- someone called [INAUDIBLE] who wrote a book about the ghetto.
She died now.
She came once-- they came once.
So that was my-- they used to come, usually.
I mean, when they were late, I would be sort of terrified because I knew nobody-- didn't know-- didn't know anybody's address.
I felt-- I was completely at the mercy of those people who, you know, and they were really having the-- I don't know.
You know, because it was partly money anyway.
I don't know what they would have done.
Under the Warsaw uprisal I stayed there.
No there-- there was Warsaw uprising--
A year later in '44, in September.
That was the-- that was the-- the Polish uprising.
Because it was-- you know, more or less then, or at least the Russians had come to the Vistula, more or less outside of Warsaw.
And before that, the Germans seemed to be sort of in disarray, leaving, going east.
I-- I could see them all, columns of them, going-- going west, rather, going-- running away.
And some Italians [? orders, ?] I remember.
But-- and I think they were badly hoping that the Russians would come in.
But the Russians wouldn't or didn't.
And the Germans eventually won this fight.
By that time, Warsaw was really in-- was in ruins.
I remember sort of hiding, being in the cellar because the rest of the building was crashed down.
And how I also had to be-- was surprised that even there, you know, people would sort of whisper that I was Jewish and so on at that time.
And-- and also [?
Vladka's ?] husband's family were-- he brought his family-- just because it seemed the end of the war somehow-- there to the same block of flats.
And they were his parents and the very-- his very young sister who was my age or a little younger even.
Suddenly there was a Jewish family.
They were-- seemed terribly Jewish.
They didn't even speak Polish particularly well, the parents.
And they burned candles.
It just made, you know, such a different experience.
So they were in one of the cellars during the uprising as well.
I don't know.
[LAUGHS] Well, I suppose I was given food.
And-- and we used to go and fetch water from the Vistula.
I remember that.
Out of the--
When things were-- got a bit quiet, I suppose when you-- yes.
And I remember that, except that eventually I saw a lot of killed people.
And so on.
At first, it seemed very exciting.
But it meant that we had lost contact with [?
Vladka ?] and all this-- and the sort of underground.

Because when the Germans got-- they evacuated everyone from that bit of Warsaw where I was and took us all to outside walls that were sort of some big church where they did some kind of selecting, taking the younger men and some women to work in Germany.
And so I remember being in that church, where I sort of come to realize that-- I also remembered the conditions, in a way, in-- in the ghetto.
And that's how it seemed rather similar.
People were so terrified.
And there were no lavatories and-- in this church.
And people were behaving so badly from-- looking for a room for each other, which I-- because I used to-- but, you know, some people behaved terribly well.
Well, people were pushing really to sit down.
And push-- and then-- and from my point of view, I-- somebody got up, and-- and-- to-- to go somewhere.
I don't know where.
And I sat down in their place.
And there were people I knew who had been quite nice me, sort of in this [INAUDIBLE].
And when this woman came back, and kind of looked at me, I was probably sitting in her-- sort of began to shout that I was Jewish.
She obviously knew all this time.
But hadn't-- you know-- and-- so they [INAUDIBLE] and a lot of people were suddenly [? became ?] sort of saying almost-- something about it.
But in fact, they didn't.
And in fact, you know, people [INAUDIBLE] sort of quieting down.
But I remember that maybe I shouldn't.
I sort of [INAUDIBLE] under stress and strain.
Yes.
This was called [INAUDIBLE].
Is that it?
That's what I-- well I think [?
Zerimba ?] was my surname.
And I was supposed to be the-- some cousin from Pomerania.
Because that part of Poland was under some different German occupation, some different system.
And there wasn't much contact.
So people couldn't check up so easily.
I think the underground.
Yes.
Perhaps [INAUDIBLE].
From-- so we-- so some people, the young-- young people, were sort of put-- taken somewhere to-- to-- and then I remember people wanting to be my-- be with me because I was the child.
And if you-- they had a child, they would probably not be sent to Germany.
A few people volunteering to be my mommy or daddy, suddenly.
[LAUGHS] But we were put on the train.
Families, mainly.
And-- the train-- I don't know where the train was going.
It stopped somewhere for minutes.
And then this family, the Serafins, Jan and Krisya were there names, and the child.
And they jumped off with me, and some other people jumped off.
And I don't think the Germans cared that much.
And we walked.
The had family in their place, called [INAUDIBLE], called [INAUDIBLE].
I think, where there was a whole family, a whole village, more or less, of people with their surname, called the Serafins.
And I-- we just walked.
And there we got and-- and stayed in this village in a little hut, a little wooden hut with-- with the family.
And somehow they let us stay there.
They-- and we ate whatever little food they had.
So that was by that time.
I suppose October.
Yes.
There were some relations.
Not-- in some [INAUDIBLE].
I think that he called her an aunt.
I don't know if she was actually his aunt or-- and they used to go.
This is [INAUDIBLE] Serafin.
The man who-- with whom I stayed was [INAUDIBLE] he and his wife used to go to Warsaw.
Walked, it was about 30 miles, I should imagine.
And find things and sort of sell some things of people who either had left or were killed or something and then bring it and sell it on the market, and the market.
But the village was so poor.
I was-- you know, after all that I had been through, I was surprised, suddenly, at Warsaw, that they'd never seen electricity.
You know, they had a well.
And they couldn't read and it was just that the-- it was such a different life, not completely self-sufficient.
Well, because of the war, they made their own clothes.
And it was winter.
I was there.
And, you know, the animals lived in the hut with them.
But looking back on it, they were quite-- it wasn't so easy to be-- it-- it really wasn't-- it was very good of them to really share their food with me.
They were not paid.
There was no contact with anybody.
Yes, yes.
Well, I [? would ?] because I have for so long, you know, remember the bad things.
And suddenly I think, well, would I have done it?
[LAUGHS] It was very risky.
And-- and quite apart from everything, they had so little food for their own children, that they shared what they had with me and I was a complete stranger.
They knew nothing about me.
Yes, I was frightened of them because at some point I realized that they guessed that I was Jewish.
I was trying to sort of say that my father was some important person in the underground.
And that, you know, they would probably get money after the war.
I never told them I was Jewish.
But I sort of realized at some point when they shouted at me, that they-- they guessed that I was Jewish.
